title: Quiz 1 — Core Foundations
questions: Define intrinsic value in your own words. What exactly is being measured, and why does this definition change how you should approach an investment decision?

A company reports ₹80 crore in net profit this year, and a friend says the company should be worth roughly ₹800 crore (10× earnings). What is wrong with this reasoning, and what would you actually need to know to estimate intrinsic value?
Explain margin of safety in your own words. Why does Buffett argue that precision in valuation is unnecessary — even counterproductive — if you have a genuinely wide margin of safety?
A stock is down 45% from its 52-week high. A colleague argues this automatically gives it a margin of safety. Do you agree? Walk through your reasoning carefully.
What structural advantage does a longer time horizon give an individual investor over most institutional fund managers? Why can't most professionals simply choose to adopt a 10-year view?

Amazon's stock fell 90% between 1999 and 2001 while the business was simultaneously building real long-term economic value. What does this episode reveal about the relationship between price and intrinsic value? What would a rational long-horizon investor have done differently?

You are analyzing a consumer goods company with a dominant brand, stable demand, minimal capital requirements, and 30 years of consistent market leadership. Its stock has just fallen 25% after a disappointing quarterly earnings report. Using all three concepts from this quiz — intrinsic value, margin of safety, and time horizon — explain how you would think about this situation.
